metadata.title: Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens: A computational text analysis of newspaper data

abstract: Following the civil war in Syria in 2011, migration from Syria in massive numbers has caused various political, demographic, societal, and cultural challenges in Turkey. The focus of this research is on how the issue of naturalization of Syrians as Turkish citizens has been presented in Turkish newspapers and to what extent the dominant perspective on the issue has shifted over time. For our computational textual analysis of Turkish newspaper materials published between 2012 and 2019, we compiled a corpus consisting of 15,276 news items from four newspapers featuring the co-occurrence of the words "Syria*" (Suriye*) and "citizen*" (vatandas ¸*) in the main body of their text. The newspapers include mainstream and progovernment (Milliyet with liberal tendencies, Sabah with center-right tendencies), and leftwing (Sol, Evrensel) perspectives. A two-step textual analysis was conducted on the corpus. First, dictionary-based topic modeling was used to chart which broader topics Turkish citizenship of Syrians is predominantly linked to the data, and how the predominance of topics shifted over time. Second, a collocation analysis of the word "refugee" (mülteci) yields a more fine-grained picture of the specific words and expressions that tend to appear often when Syrian refugees are discussed. In both steps, an aggregated general analysis was complemented with separate newspaper-specific analyses, thus allowing for the comparison of different newspapers. Findings show that Syrians in Turkey predominantly feature in the context of diplomatic and societal topics. While mainstream news sources approach the issue less critically, left-wing newspapers show more sensitivity to migrant rights in Turkey.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: The causes of human migration vary in terms of economic, cultural, political, and environmental factors. Since the beginning of the civil war in Syria, almost 13 million Syrian citizens have been displaced (SOHR, 2021). Most of these people migrated to their neighboring countries (mainly Turkey, Lebanon, Jordan) and some followed the dangerous paths to Europe. With this wave, Turkey has been the host to the largest refugee population in the world for the last consecutive years (UNHCR, 2022a).

Turkey has provided healthcare, education, and accommodation to Syrian refugees from its own subsidies and also with the support of external funds such as those from the European Union (EU). At the same time, the increased number of Syrian refugees in Turkey during this period has also sparked divisive political and societal debates. Some of these concerns are demographic changes in cities, the prolonged time Syrians have been hosted in Turkey, and whether they will be granted Turkish citizenship. It is argued that if the time spent in the host country increases, more citizenship take-up starts (OECD and European Union, 2015).

Because the Syrian refugees in Turkey have been under 'temporary protection status' (explained more in detail in the Political Context section), their acquisition of Turkish citizenship is contentious due to the nature of this status. However, in 2016, President Recep Tayyip Erdo gan announced that Syrians in Turkey would obtain Turkish citizenship during a fast-breaking meal (iftar) in the border city of Kilis (which hosts a majority of Syrians relative to the percentage of the city population).

"Tonight, I want to give good news to my siblings here. I believe that among our siblings there are those who wish to become citizens of the Republic of Turkey. We will give these siblings the opportunity of citizenship by following their best assistance with an office created by our Ministry." (Sabah, 3 July 2016) His statement sparked many discussions and had broad repercussions in the national and international media (BBC Turkish, 2016;Cumhuriyet, 2016;Milliyet, 2016;Reuters, 2016). International media also drew attention to Erdogan's move, sometimes analyzing it as a ploy geared to drawing loyal new voters from the Syrian community. Especially during the 2018 General Elections in Turkey, Syrians who had recently been granted citizenship were viewed as potential voters for Erdogan's Justice and Development Party (Pitel, 2016;Smith, 2018). In a similar vein, concerns from the opposition parties and the public have been raised in Turkey.

Regarding mass media's central role in everyday politics, it is indispensable that media reflect and inform public opinion (Young and Soroka, 2012). In this light, this study aims to investigate the distribution of topics in the news items about the naturalization of Syrians over time and trends depending on the news source. Secondly, collocation analysis reveals how Syrian refugees are contextualized in the different segments of Turkish media. This research asks the following questions.

Research question 1: How do the topics discussed about granting Turkish citizenship to Syrians differ over time? Are there any differences in topic distributions depending on the news source (newspaper)? Research question 2: What are the most common related words of "refugee" in Turkish media? Do they change depending on the news source (newspaper)?

This study presents a combination of migration, media and linguistics studies through computational data analysis lenses by focusing on the analysis of a newspaper data set. Previous work on this topic, as discussed in the next section, has mostly relied on smallscale data with less concentration on computational methods. In this study, with topic modeling, we aimed at identifying the broader topics and domains in the context of which the combination of 'Syria' and 'citizenship' mostly occurs. Therefore, it helps to identify how this combination of words enters into news items and what kind of information (economic, political, societal, etc.) is deemed newsworthy and receives media attention. Collocation analysis, on the other hand, informs the reader about how the text in the corpus talks about a specific word/term ("refugee" in this study) on a more fine-grained level, together with its collocates. This method offers different types of information because there can be many reasons why a word shows up as a collocate of the term, e.g., stylistic reasons, specific events that are often referred to, or common (cultural) connotations and associations.

This paper is organized as follows. First, related work in the fields of migration and media will be discussed. Next, the political context regarding the status of Syrians in Turkey and the media structure in Turkey will be briefly explained together with the theoretical base. After introducing the methodology, the study's findings in the analysis will be presented, followed by discussions. We conclude with a general interpretation of the findings and with some final remarks on the limitations of this study and as well as possibilities for future work.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: The research on Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens has been conducted in a variety of contexts involving qualitative and quantitative methods. While some scholars have presented more theoretical perspectives (Koser-Akcapar and Simsek, 2018;Tümtas ¸, 2018;Yıldız, 2017), others have relied on analyses based on media data from various sources. The qualitative studies with media data concerning Syrians in Turkey include the works of Göktuna Yaylacı and Karakus ¸(2015) who analyzed a 1-year period of media data in 2014; of Onay-Coker (2019) who analyzed a 4-month period in 2017 with over 250 news items; of Atasü-Topçuoglu (2019) who also analyzed over 250 news items over the period of 1 year; of Bilge (2019) by analyzing 3260 news items from five newspapers; and of Sert and Danıs ¸(2021) who discuss the crisis discourse created for addressing Syrian refugees between 2015 and 2017 in 250 news items.

A few quantitative studies have been conducted on relatively larger data. While Sunata and Yıldız (2018) investigated 1054 news items over a 3-month period in 2015, Efe (2019) conducted a corpus-driven analysis of over 2300 news items in WordSmith 7 between the years 2011 and 2016 by focusing on concordances but not differences depending on newspapers. Several studies also addressed comparative country cases (Dimitrova et al., 2018;Ozdora Aksak et al., 2021).

Some research has also focused on social media data regarding opinions towards Syrian refugees in Turkey. For instance, Öztürk and Ayvaz (2018) analyzed the sentiments of 348,707 tweets in Turkish and English about the Syrian refugee crisis for a period of 1 month in 2017. Bozdag (2020), Erdogan-Ozturk and Isik-Guler (2020), and Özerim and Tolay (2021) specifically zoomed in on anti-Syrian and discriminative discourse towards Syrian refugees in Turkish tweets (such as #BuMisafirlikFazlaUzadı-#ThisGuestshipOverextended).

The most common findings of these studies include the increased perception of a threat towards Syrian refugees in Turkish public and the discontent about Turkey's migration management policies. Syrian refugees have been depicted in two contrasting framesas both victimized and stranded between being poor or needy and as a threat to Turkish society. According to Efe (2019), the Turkish media represents Syrian refugees as a "potential threat to the security, cultural integrity and economy of the country". In Turkey, the ways news is represented is also an important factor in conveying information and creating public perceptions, which highly depends on the political and ideological stance of the newspaper.

Previous studies touched upon news media and social media discourse by analyzing data from a relatively short period of time. They addressed a few topics such as perceptions, points of discontent, and general representations regarding the issue. However, the differences depending on the news source have not been investigated. Therefore, this research aims to present a corpus-based longitudinal study by focusing on media source data and differences over time. While topic modeling helps us see in what contexts the news about Syrians is presented over time and from which news source, collocation analysis shows what meanings readers are often exposed to (O'Halloran, 2010) about Syrians in Turkey.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: The arrival of Syrians in Turkey in massive numbers has been contested in Turkey in both the political and wider public arenas. Following the uncertainty caused by the Syrian Civil War, Turkey envisaged hosting Syrians temporarily in the beginning. This temporariness has also affected the status of Syrians in Turkey, due to Turkey's specific position in the international treaties on protection. Syrians in Turkey do not officially possess refugee status, owing to Turkey's geographical restriction on the 1951 Refugee Convention. According to this limitation, Turkey grants refugee status only to those who have fled their countries of origin as a consequence of events occurring within Europe. Therefore, Syrians in Turkey are under the status of 'temporary protection', which is regulated under the 6458 Law on Foreigners and International Protection ( 2013) and Temporary Protection Regulation (2014). The main principles of temporary protection status are acceptance to Turkey with the open border policy, implementation of the non-refoulement principle without exceptions, and fulfillment of the basic needs of newly arrived Syrians in Turkey (Presidency of Migration Management Turkey, 2022b). As a consequence of terminological complexity, Syrians in Turkey have been called 'refugees' (mülteciler), 'asylum seekers' (sı gınmacılar), or solely Syrians (Suriyeliler) by the media. Among these, 'refugees' is by far the most frequently used term when referring to Syrians.

Since the beginning of the civil war in Syria, the number of Syrians who migrated to Turkey increased cumulatively. As of 3 February 2022, there were 3,739,859 Syrians under temporary protection in the country (See Figure 1).

The demographic and statistical information about Syrians in Turkey is publicly announced regularly. However, the same is not provided for the number of Syrians who have acquired Turkish citizenship. The only source of information is the Ministry of Interior's irregularly announced statistics, which most recently announced in August 2022 that a total of 211,908 Syrians had been awarded Turkish citizenship so far. The irregularity of such data announcements is also a contributor to tensions among politicians and the public since they believe that the real numbers are not announced, and more Syrians obtained Turkish citizenship.

The irregularity of such data announcements is also a contributor to tensions among politicians and the public since they believe that the real numbers are not announced, and more Syrians obtained Turkish citizenship.
sections.preview_head: The arrival of Syrians in Turkey in massive numbers has been contested in Turkey in both the political and wider public arenas. Following the uncertainty caused by the Syrian Civil War, Turkey envisaged hosting Syrians temporarily in the beginning. This temporariness has also affected the status of Syrians in Turkey, due to Turkey's specific position in the international treaties on protection.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: News framing is often used in media studies to investigate how various topics are addressed in (different) media outlets. Goffman (1974) defines the news framing theory as the relationship of 'meaning' by showing how the situations are defined. In the battle of meanings, the decision to "show situation" is related to the selection of presentation of information that may potentially influence the attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors (Chong and Druckman, 2007), which later expands to a larger political and media context. Kitzinger (2007: 134) likens framing to taking a photograph: when a photograph is taken, a frame is depicted by "freezing an image of a moment from a certain perspective". The framing of an issue also often implies prioritizing certain elements of the issue and to some extent, sets an agenda which refers to the agenda-setting theory. As McCombs and Shaw (1993) underlie, object and frame selection play an important role in setting the agenda. In this study, we associate and discuss the frames within the topic modeling and collocation analysis to address certain perspectives across different newspapers. For this selection, we have been inspired by the classification of Pan and Kosicki (1993) for the news frames in media. Among their four structural dimensions (syntactical, script, thematic, rhetorical), we selected the syntactical and thematic structures in line with our methodology (topic modeling and collocation analysis). While syntactical structures are concerned with the arrangement of words we explored in collocation analysis, thematic structures focus on issues or topics associated with our choice of topic modeling.

Treating text as data, news framing can be applied computationally in three ways: lexical-based, unsupervised machine learning, and supervised machine learning (Guo et al., 2023). Among them, we followed a semi-supervised machine learning approach to achieve more relevant results in Turkish text.

paragraphs: Turkish media outlets cover a wide range of spectrums. The alignment and political affiliation of any newspaper are highly dependent on its ownership due to the liberalization steps taken in the 1980s. Especially after the 2001 economic crisis, which most affected and shaped today's media market in Turkey, some of the holdings investing in the media field, along with the financial sector, had to leave the market, while the groups that managed to stay in the market became stronger (S özeri and Güney, 2011). The ones who were removed from the market were gathered in the hands of the Savings Deposit Insurance Fund of Turkey (TMSF) and then put up for sale through tenders over time. Over time media ownership in Turkey has been polarized and transformed into a venue of party-press parallelism (Iseri et al., 2019). In sum, as Furman et al. (2019) point out, media in Turkey can be divided into three categories: corporate, private, and non-profit.

The newspapers in our corpus represent different political alignments. Milliyet (Nationality), with its ownership history from Dogan Group to DK Gazetecilik, has been a pro-government mainstream newspaper with liberal tendencies (Haede, 2013). Sabah (Morning), owned by the Kalyon Group, is also a representative of the pro-government mainstream news source with more center-right tendencies (Bayar and Kotelis, 2014). Being pro-government in the present Turkish context means having center-right tendencies and conservative values.

On the other side of the spectrum, Sol (Left) and Evrensel (Universal) are positioned as the representatives of left-wing and socialist perspectives. While Evrensel is owned by an individual (Kürs ¸at Yılmaz) who does not have close ties with the government, Sol is owned by a leftist publishing company called Gelenek. In 2014, Sol terminated its print edition and since then has continued as an online news portal. In total, the newspapers in this corpus are both from corporate and private initiatives. Regarding the circulation results or print-run of these newspapers, Sabah has 304,008, Milliyet has 156,625, Sol has 20,694, and Evrensel has 13,685 (Gazeteciler, 2012).

paragraphs: The words of texts convey information, discourses, ideologies, and perspectives. Computational analysis of text not only helps save time in the analysis, it also expands the "efficiency, scope, and reliability" of the research on a larger scale (Young and Soroka, 2012). In light of this, this study applies topic modeling and collocation analysis to newspaper text data in order to extract meanings and trends about the naturalization of Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: The data for this research was extracted from a large newspaper dataset (Kurnaz, 2022) consisting of 2.79 million news items between 2012 and 2019 in Turkey. The extraction criteria were having the co-occurrences of the words "Suriye*" (Syria*) and "vatandas ¸*" (citizen*) in the news body part of four selected newspapers (Milliyet, Sabah, Sol, Evrensel). The reason for selecting these four newspapers was to obtain and analyze a wider array of perspectives and for their data availability. Due to their having comparatively fewer news items and their close similar ideological stances, news items from Sol and Evrensel are merged for the analysis. They are jointly called "Sol and Evrensel" in this study. Sabah and Milliyet on the other hand, despite being in the mainstream cluster, were analyzed separately due to their slightly different characteristics (Milliyet as liberal, Sabah as center-right). The entire dataset consisting of 15,276 news items is called "the corpus".

Considering that the automatically collected data might involve some noise (Yantseva, 2020), the corpus also has some news items that might not directly address the naturalization issue, albeit containing the co-occurrences of the words "Syria*" and "citizen*". Despite such instances, the corpus still provides an efficient base for the scope of this research. Figure 2 shows the number of news items in the corpus by year.
The increment of news items in 2016 is due to Erdogan's statement of granting Turkish citizenship to Syrians on 2 July 2016 as his statement received wide coverage in the media that year.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: Roberts et al. ( 2019) define a topic as a combination of words where every word holds the probability of belonging to a topic. Topic modeling, as a combination of different disciplines, aims at clustering the words in a document into topics "to tell a compelling story of the text to support hypotheses" (Ramage et al., 2009: 1) or discover meanings. Topic modeling has been used in media studies to explore various research areas; such as analyzing social media discourse toward Muslims (Törnberg and Törnberg, 2016), and discussing the nuclear technology debate (Jacobi et al., 2016).

In this research, to find the most discussed topics in newspapers, topic modeling is applied as the first unsupervised attempt. Based on the classification results of this attempt (depending on the user's preferred number of topics), the topics were not classified as clearly as expected for the scope of this research. This led us to the conclusion that the algorithm requires guidance from user input in order to decompose the corpus to the relevant topics. Therefore, we abandoned the unsupervised approach and turned to a semisupervised technique.

To classify the topics more relevantly, a dictionary-based topic modeling (as a semisupervised model) was applied to predefine topics with keywords that can facilitate textual analysis. User-defined dictionaries provide a set of keywords in predefined categories and correspond to certain concepts, while keywords (also called 'seed words') refer to the "words that are used to train semi-supervised document classifiers" (Watanabe and Zhou, 2022).

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
paragraphs: Collocation analysis is used to find "the co-occurrence frequencies of word pairs that are ranked according to some quantitative measure of their statistical association" (Evert, 2016: 1). Conducting collocation analysis in media text reveals "what meanings readers are regularly exposed to, and the frames they may possess" (O'Halloran, 2010: 570). In this study, the analysis is performed with keywords considering their role in identifying important elements of the text (Bondi, 2010). A window is determined to identify related words of keywords based on their distance in the documents and to compare the "frequency of words inside and outside their context" (Benoit et al., 2018). The result of the collocation analysis shows a list of collocates with their collocational strength scores measured as the so-called chi-squared test statistic.

paragraphs: The analysis of this study was performed in RStudio. Several packages of R were used for the analysis, with the quanteda (Benoit et al., 2018) and STM (structural topic model) (Roberts et al., 2019) packages proving the most beneficial. Below, two methods (topic modeling and collocation analysis) are applied to the corpus, and their findings are given accompanied by their explanations, as well as visualizations with English translations.

sections.header_raw: Topic distributions over time and across newspapers
sections.header_norm: topic distributions over time and across newspapers
paragraphs: To explore the topic distributions, a dictionary is created as a semi-supervised step for predefining topics in the Turkish text with five topics (Table 1) by taking the example dictionaries of Lu et al. (2011) and Blei et al. (2003). The five topics in the dictionary consist of main news categories (similar to the study of Sjøvaag and Stavelin, 2012) in the mediaeconomy, politics, society, diplomacy, and militaryand their seed words are assigned according to the Turkish context. While several seed words are given for each topic to serve introductory purposes, the algorithm also affiliates and adds other words related to each topic. After applying the topic modeling dictionary, more relevant clusters of terms emerged for each topic. Figure 3 displays the most frequent five terms in each topic with their phi coefficients, which is a measure used for the probability of the word in the topic. The terms in each topic are highly interrelated among themselves and also correspond to the specific context here, of the Syrian case in Turkey. For instance, the topic of society frequently contains many specific migration-related terms, as well as 'misafir' (guest), which relates to the "guest discourse" created by Turkey to draw attention to Turkish hospitality towards them and also by aiming at easing tensions in society highlighting the temporariness of the case. This term has also been used in social media debates mostly negatively to address Syrians hosted by Turkey. Under the topic military, top terms relate to NATO operations and terrorist incidents in Turkey and Syria. Under diplomacy, meanwhile, international organizations such as the United Nations and signatures of treaties appear more frequently. As expected, under politics, voting-related terms occur more frequently. More specifically, 'referendum' and 'kılıçdaroglu' (the main opposition party [CHP] leader) refer to the criticism raised against Syrians' acquisition of Turkish citizenship as illustrated in the news excerpts below.

Kılıçdaroglu, who proposed a referendum on granting citizenship to Syrians and the Raqqa operation, said: Now, they will be granted citizenship. If desired, we should go to a referendum. They always say that the public knows better. Our citizens turned into second-class citizens in their own country. (Milliyet, 28 February 2017) Ministry of Interior Soylu said: An issue that has become a cliché is the Syrians' voting issue. In Turkey, there are 3,632,622 Syrians with international protection status. Not every Syrian you see on the street has the right to vote, such a thing is not possible. (Sabah, 20 January 2019) Regarding the overall distribution of topics in the corpus (Figure 4), diplomacy and society are those that appear most frequently, while economy is the least discussed. The high proportion of diplomacy-related topics in the corpus is linked to the international scope (as many actors involved such as UN, EU, and NATO) of the migration from Syria, as is also seen in the top terms of the topic diplomacy. Military is followed by society when region-related operations are considered.

As seen in Figure 5, while the topic of politics was discussed considerably around the beginning of the period in 2012-13, it then gradually appeared far less frequently, although it rose quite sharply again in 2018. These changes in politics and diplomacy are related to the ramification of the issue over time. Similarly, at the start of the period, diplomacy shared the highest frequency, but later it leveled off at around 30-40%. On the other hand, the topic military underwent a significant increase due to the cross-border military operations carried out by Turkey in the Northern parts of Syriamore specifically, Operation Euphrates Shield (Fırat Kalkanı Harekâtı) and Operation Olive Branch (Zeytin Dalı Harekâtı) in 2016 and 2018 respectively. The reflections of these operations were discussed and criticized in the Turkish media for a long time. The topic society remained more or less the same throughout the period due to the dominance of military operations. Despite displaying a similar trend to the topic of society regarding the fluctuations, the topic of economy began to show a steady increase after 2017 because of growing affiliations of the issue with economic concerns while the topic of society did not follow such an increase.
For a more fine-grained analysis addressing differences among newspapers, each newspaper was also analyzed according to topic distributions over time (Figure 6).

In the topics economy and diplomacy, all newspapers seem to share an overall similar trend. On the other hand, Sol and Evrensel clearly tend to feature more politicsrelated news compared to the other two. It can be related to the selection criteria of Sol and Evrensel considering their political orientation. While the topic society reaches its peak in 2015 for Milliyet, and Sol and Evrensel, its peak in Sabah happens in 2019 which can both be caused by having fewer news items or a slight change in the news coverage. 2015 was the year when the number of Syrians in Turkey increased by one million compared to the previous year (See Figure 1). For Milliyet, the topic society can be considered as one of the most discussed topics due to its overall high proportion. Milliyet's coverage prefers to distinguish from Sabah in this regard. The topic military only shows a peak in Milliyet in 2019, while other newspapers show a downward trend in 2019.

sections.paper_id: Istif Inci and Speelman - 2025 - Syrians in Turkey and their naturalization as Turkish citizens A computational text analysis of new
sections.header_raw: The most common collates of 'refugee' in the newspapers
sections.header_norm: the most common collates of refugee in the newspapers
paragraphs: As certain uses of the chosen words can provide insights about social and cultural preoccupations (Gerbig, 2010), in this study, "refugee" (mülteci) is selected as the keyword when its salient usage in the Turkish context is considered. A 5-word window is applied around the keyword in the corpus to map closer related words as part of the collocation analysis.

The keyword "refugee" is analyzed in the three (considering that Sol and Evrensel are merged) news sources individually to investigate and compare different trends. The top twenty related words of "refugee" around a 5-window are visualized in Figure 7. Starting with Milliyet and Sabah, it is observed that diplomacy-related features occur more often. The role of international organizations such as the UNHCR (United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees), EU, and UNRWA (United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East) takes more place in the issue. Commissioner is also related to persons as seen in "antonio" (last collocate in Sabah) which refers to Antonio Guterres, who worked as the UN High Commissioner for Refugees between 2005 and 2015 and has been the Secretary-General of the UN since 2017. Guterres monitored the humanitarian acts for Syrian refugees and conducted visits to Turkey, mainly to refugee camps. The occurrence of "askı" (suspension), "kabul" (admission)" and "ab" (eu) is related to the EU-Turkey Deal that was signed between the European Council and Turkey on 18 March 2016 aiming to stop irregular migration flow from Turkey to Europe. According to the deal, (also called "the refugee agreement" in the media), Syrian refugees who do not apply for asylum after reaching Greece illegally or whose application is rejected are sent back to Turkey, and for every Syrian returned, the EU committed to accept one Syrian refugee from the camps in Turkey. It also projected facilitating visa liberalization for Turkish citizens in the Schengen area -and promised progress in the EU membership negotiations for Turkey. However, the deal has been suspended in 2019. As "camp" is listed in the top three in every newspaper, it is highly linked to the "refugee camp" (mülteci kampı). While Syrian refugees were staying in camps in the first years of the migration wave, over time they mobilized to the cities. Therefore, each year a certain number of camps were closed down (Milliyet, 2018). In 2022, only 1.33% of Syrian refugees in Turkey stay in camps in the cities of Adana, Hatay, Kahramanmaras ¸, Kilis and Osmaniye (Presidency of Migration Management Turkey, 2022b).

Sol and Evrensel have more diplomacy-related terms and the names of political figures who have weighed in on the question of migration, notably Tanju Özcan ('tanju'), the mayor of Bolu, who is known for his anti-immigrant statements and decisions. His actions have been criticized by journalists and party politicians including his affiliated CHP (Republican People's Party).

A dangerous directive came from Tanju Özcan, who was elected Mayor of Bolu. He sent a letter to the Bolu Municipality Culture and Social Affairs Directorate with the words "No financial and inkind aid is given to foreign nationals living in our province". (Evrensel, 9 April 2019) Another proper noun in the collocation analysis findings of Sol and Evrensel is "terzi". In this context, it refers to the President of the Bridging Peoples Association, Cem Terzi. Terzi is an advocate of migrant rights and regularly makes press statements to support granting refugee status to Syrians in Turkey. While Milliyet and Sabah did not give place to Terzi, Sol and Evrensel frequently made news of his statements as one of them is stated below.

Terzi said, "When refugee status is granted, these people will preserve their identity and have the potential to return to their own country. They will have continued to be a political subject. When you dissolve them under the discourse of citizenship, you eliminate their expectations of their own country, their future, their state of being a political subject." (Evrensel, 10 July 2016) Sol and Evrensel also draw attention to the integration side of migration as the feature "entegrasyon" (integration) took place in the top twenty. All these can lead to the conclusion that the left-wing perspectives in Turkish media are more sensitive to legalizing and securing the rights of migrants.

Finally, "ege" (aegean) and "akdeniz" (mediterranean) are related to the irregular migration route to Europe from Turkey where many tragic incidents have occurred. Unsafe sea travel conditions on this route together with land crossings (to Greece and Bulgaria) and human smugglers put refugees' lives at risk. According to the UNHCR (2022b), 2051 refugees have been dead and missing between 2014 and 2020 in Greece and its territorial waters.

paragraphs: The computational analysis of media text revealed that Syrian refugees in Turkey and their acquisition of Turkish citizenship are mostly framed in diplomatic and societal topics in Turkish media. The relation with diplomacy is linked with the international structure of the so-called "refugee crisis", whereas society is mostly about the debates concerning the demographic changes and concerns in Turkey.

Theoretically, news framing as investigated in the syntactical and thematic structures of Pan and Kosicki (1993) guided our study to ground its base. Syntactical structures were explored in the collocations of "refugee" which revealed how left-wing media clearly distinguished from the mainstream media in approaching Syrians in Turkey. Thematic structures showed the frames which were classified as topics both cumulatively and specific for each newspaper.

On political alignment, while pro-government sources shared similar stances as seen in their topic distributions and collocates, left-wing perspectives focused more on the rights-based approaches. This difference was mostly observed in collocates as Sol and Evrensel had distinctive words, e.g. "hak" (right) and "entegrasyon" (integration) were only in the collocate list of Sol and Evrensel. Another major difference among the newspapers was the "statü" (status) being the most common in Sol and Evrensel, considering that left-wing sources mostly framed Syrians' dilemma of not being able to possess refugee status. A similar finding was also stated in Atasü-Topçuo gu's (2019) research, where she observed that left-wing media perspectives supported legalizing and securing the rights of Syrians in Turkey.

On the other hand, pro-government Milliyet and Sabah shared common tendencies in conveying information (with slight changes), considering Sabah being more center-right and its ties with the government.

Another aspect emerging from the analyses is related to the Europe-related structure of the issue, since in each newspaper, collocations of "refugee" were frequently associated with Europe. This relation derives from the contested EU-Turkey Deal, as Syrians in Turkey and those trying to cross borders to reach Europe from Turkey have been a matter of negotiation between both parties during the entire period. The prevalence of the topic diplomacy in the overall topic modeling results also supports these events.

The increasing number of Syrian refugees in Turkey since 2012 has triggered many concerns and discussions across the public, media and political spheres. This increase has brought with it immense administrative and societal challenges for Turkey. The naturalization of Syrians as Turkish citizens continues to become a major concern, as it represents the transformation from being temporary to permanent and thus potentially eliminates the "guest discourse" (Presidency of Migration Management Turkey, 2022). It draws more attention to the integration regulations and efforts that need to be taken by policymakers to ease tensions and create an inclusive society. Since media is a domain where different social groups and ideologies try to construct their 'desired' social reality (Gurevitch and Levy (1985), media's role in Turkey appears to contribute to the debate in the way information is conveyed and perceptions are created, albeit being highly dependent on the political alignment of the news source.

On a methodological side, while topic modeling and collocation analysis allowed us to discover systematic patterns in large quantities of textual data inductively (Lind et al., 2021), there were some challenges working on the Turkish text. Specifically, in topic modeling, dictionary-based analysis has proven to be more suitable for classifying Turkish text rather than the unsupervised model.

He also is chair of the Leuven Statistics Research Centre (LStat) and member of the KU Leuven Institute for Artificial Intelligence (Leuven.AI). Dirk's main research interest lies in the fields of corpus linguistics, computational lexicology and variational linguistics in general. Much of his work focuses on methodology and on the application of statistical and other quantitative methods to the study of language. Some topics that he currently works on are distributional semantics and its application to the study op language variation, quantitative measures in support of aggregate level analyses of lexical variation, and statistical methods and machine learning in support of research into syntactic variation.
